```{r US SPREAD HY RATING TXT, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
    'The ICE BofA BB US High Yield Index OAS covers the highest-quality tier of the HY universe, just below investment grade. BB-rated issuers are often crossover names, fallen angels from IG or rising stars approaching IG, and their spreads are particularly sensitive to rating migration risk and relative value dynamics between the IG and HY markets.',
    '<br><br>',
    '3) <b>B — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLH0A2HYB" target="_blank">BAMLH0A2HYB</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA Single-B US High Yield Index OAS covers the core of the leveraged credit market, encompassing companies with meaningful leverage and limited financial flexibility. Single-B spreads are driven by a blend of default risk, recovery rate expectations, and broader risk appetite, and widen significantly more than BB spreads during credit stress episodes as investors price in higher default probabilities.',
    '<br><br>',
    '4) <b>CCC & Lower — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLH0A3HYC" target="_blank">BAMLH0A3HYC</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA CCC & Lower US High Yield Index OAS covers the most distressed tier of the corporate credit universe, including bonds rated CCC, CC, C, and D. Issuers in this tier face high default probabilities and limited market access. CCC spreads are the most volatile in all of corporate credit and serve as the most direct market-implied gauge of near-term default cycle risk.'
  ),
  interpretation = "The four HY rating tranches together tell the full story of the speculative grade credit cycle. In benign conditions, all tranches tighten and the CCC-to-BB differential stays compressed as broad risk appetite supports even the most distressed issuers. As the cycle turns, CCC spreads widen first and most violently, followed by Single-B and then BB, reflecting the sequential repricing of default risk from the most vulnerable issuers outward. The all-HY index, being a weighted average, tends to lag the most distressed tranches but leads the broader market as a summary risk signal. A spread above 500–600 basis points on the all-HY index has historically signalled recessionary credit conditions. The CCC-to-BB ratio is one of the most powerful real-time indicators of how far into the default cycle markets believe we are, with a rapidly widening ratio typically preceding a pick-up in actual default rates by several months.")
```

```{=html}
<div class="perf-section">
```

```{r US SPREAD HY RATING, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
data <- get_series(name = c("ice_bofa_allhy_us", "ice_bofa_bb_us", "ice_bofa_b_us", "ice_bofa_ccc_us"))
div(style = "display: flex; justify-content: center;",
    produce_interactive_plot(df = data, sma = FALSE, title = "US HY Corporate OAS by Rating, All HY, BB, B, CCC & Lower"))
```

```{=html}
</div>
```

#### *Spreads By Maturity*

```{r US SPREAD IG MAT TXT, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
series_info_box(
  source_html = 'FRED — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/" target="_blank">link</a>',
  frequency = "Daily",
  adjustment = "Not Adjusted",
  about = paste0(
    '1) <b>1–3 Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC1A0C13Y" target="_blank">BAMLC1A0C13Y</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 1-3 Year US Corporate Index OAS covers short-dated IG bonds, the most liquid segment of the corporate market. These spreads are highly sensitive to near-term credit conditions, monetary policy expectations, and short-term funding dynamics, and are closely watched as a gauge of corporate refinancing risk at the short end.',
    '<br><br>',
    '2) <b>3–5 Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC2A0C35Y" target="_blank">BAMLC2A0C35Y</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 3-5 Year US Corporate Index OAS sits at the transition between short and medium-term credit risk. It reflects a blend of near-term fundamental conditions and medium-term macro expectations, and widening relative to the 1-3 year bucket often signals the start of a broader spread widening cycle.',
    '<br><br>',
    '3) <b>5–7 Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC3A0C57Y" target="_blank">BAMLC3A0C57Y</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 5-7 Year US Corporate Index OAS sits at the heart of the medium-term IG market, balancing interest rate duration risk with fundamental credit risk. It is closely monitored by portfolio managers navigating allocation decisions between short and long credit.',
    '<br><br>',
    '4) <b>7–10 Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC4A0C710Y" target="_blank">BAMLC4A0C710Y</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 7-10 Year US Corporate Index OAS is one of the most actively traded and benchmarked segments of the IG corporate market, widely used as the primary reference for new bond issuance and a key driver of total return volatility in IG portfolios.',
    '<br><br>',
    '5) <b>10–15 Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC7A0C1015Y" target="_blank">BAMLC7A0C1015Y</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 10-15 Year US Corporate Index OAS covers long-dated IG bonds primarily accessed by liability-driven investors such as pension funds and insurance companies. Spreads here are highly sensitive to structural changes in long-term inflation expectations, fiscal outlooks, and institutional buyer behaviour.',
    '<br><br>',
    '6) <b>15+ Years — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLC8A0C15PY" target="_blank">BAMLC8A0C15PY</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA 15+ Year US Corporate Index OAS covers the ultra-long end of the IG market, carrying the highest duration risk within the investment grade universe. It is the most sensitive maturity bucket to term premium dynamics and structural demand from institutions with very long-dated liabilities, and exhibits the largest total return swings across the IG maturity spectrum.'
  ),
  interpretation = "Reading the six maturity buckets together reveals the term structure of IG corporate credit spreads and how credit and duration risk are priced across the curve. In a normal credit environment, spreads are upward sloping across maturities, longer buckets command more spread, reflecting the higher uncertainty and duration risk of longer-dated cash flows. When the credit spread curve flattens or inverts at longer maturities, it typically signals strong institutional demand for long-duration assets or a market pricing in a benign long-term credit outlook. Conversely, a steepening of the spread curve at the long end signals rising term premium concerns, deteriorating long-term credit outlooks, or a reduction in structural buyer demand. Watching the dynamic between the short end (1-3Y) and the long end (15+Y) provides a real-time read on the shape of the IG credit curve and how duration risk is being priced relative to near-term credit risk.")
```

```{=html}
<div class="perf-section">
```

```{r US SPREAD IG MAT, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
data <- get_series(name = c("ice_bofa_1_3y_us", "ice_bofa_3_5y_us", "ice_bofa_5_7y_us", "ice_bofa_7_10y_us", "ice_bofa_10_15y_us", "ice_bofa_15py_us"))
div(style = "display: flex; justify-content: center;",
    produce_interactive_plot(df = data, sma = FALSE, title = "US IG Corporate OAS by Maturity — 1-3Y, 3-5Y, 5-7Y, 7-10Y, 10-15Y, 15+Y"))
```

```{=html}
</div>
```

### BofA Index

```{r BOFA IDX TXT, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
series_info_box(
  source_html = 'ICE BoFa — <a href="https://fr.finance.yahoo.com/quote/%5EMOVE/" target="_blank">link</a>',
  frequency = "Daily",
  adjustment = "Not Adjusted",
  about = "ICE BofA MOVE Index (^MOVE) measures the implied volatility of U.S. Treasury bonds across the yield curve, published by ICE Data Indices and derived from the prices of one-month over-the-counter options on U.S. Treasury securities across four maturities (2-year, 5-year, 10-year, and 30-year) weighted by the outstanding amount of each maturity in the Treasury market. The resulting composite reflects the bond market\'s consensus expectation of near-term interest rate volatility, making the MOVE the fixed income equivalent of the VIX, which measures equity market implied volatility and earning it the informal designation of the bond market fear gauge. Elevated MOVE readings indicate that options markets are pricing significant uncertainty about the future path of interest rates, typically occurring during periods of monetary policy uncertainty, inflation surprises, fiscal stress, or broader financial market dislocation; low readings reflect calm rate expectations and stable monetary policy conditions. Because interest rate volatility transmits directly to borrowing costs, mortgage rates, credit spreads, and asset valuations across virtually every corner of the financial system, the MOVE is closely watched as a leading indicator of financial conditions tightness and systemic stress, often anticipating broader market dislocations before they materialize in equity volatility measures. Quoted in basis points of annualized yield volatility,updated daily with closing prices.",
  interpretation = "The MOVE Index functions as the bond market's fear gauge, translating options pricing on U.S. Treasuries into a single composite measure of expected near-term interest rate volatility across the yield curve. Because it is derived from one-month OTC options spanning the 2Y, 5Y, 10Y, and 30Y maturities and weighted by outstanding issuance, it captures the market's aggregate uncertainty about the future path of rates rather than any single point on the curve. In tranquil monetary environments, MOVE readings remain subdued, reflecting stable rate expectations and predictable Fed policy, providing a low-volatility backdrop that compresses credit spreads, tightens financial conditions, and supports risk asset valuations broadly. Spikes in the MOVE signal that options markets are repricing rate uncertainty sharply, typically triggered by inflation surprises, unexpected shifts in Fed communication, fiscal stress events, or broader financial dislocation, and these episodes tend to precede wider credit spreads, higher mortgage rates, and tighter lending conditions with a meaningful lead time over equity volatility measures. The transmission mechanism is direct: elevated rate volatility raises the discount rate applied to all fixed income cash flows, destabilizes duration hedging for institutional portfolios, and compresses the risk-taking capacity of leveraged participants, making elevated MOVE readings an early warning signal of systemic stress even when equity markets appear calm. Watching the MOVE alongside credit spread levels creates a powerful joint signal, when both are elevated simultaneously it typically marks peak financial stress, while a declining MOVE paired with still-wide spreads often signals the early stages of a credit spread compression cycle as rate uncertainty resolves before credit risk premium fully normalizes.")
```

```{=html}
<div class="perf-section">
```

```{r VSTOXX, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}

# Get series
data <- get_series(name = "move_idx")

# Compute sma 
data <- compute_moving_average(df = data, date_col = "date", value_col = "value")

# Plot serie
div(style = "display: flex; justify-content: center;",
    produce_interactive_plot(df = data, sma = TRUE))

```

```{=html}
</div>
```


## Emerging Markets 

### BofA Rates

#### *Spreads*

```{r EM SPREAD TXT, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
series_info_box(
  source_html = paste0(
    'Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis — ',
    '1) EM Corporate <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLEMCBPIOAS" target="_blank">BAMLEMCBPIOAS</a> · ',
    '2) EM HY Corporate <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLEMHBHYCRPIOAS" target="_blank">BAMLEMHBHYCRPIOAS</a>'
  ),
  frequency = "Daily",
  adjustment = "Not Adjusted",
  about = paste0(
    '1) <b>EM Corporate — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLEMCBPIOAS" target="_blank">BAMLEMCBPIOAS</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index OAS measures the yield spread of EM corporate bonds, spanning the full IG and HY rating spectrum, over equivalent-maturity US Treasuries, adjusted for embedded optionality. It is the primary benchmark for the EM corporate credit market and captures the combined credit risk, country risk, and liquidity risk premium embedded in bonds issued by companies domiciled in emerging market countries.',
    '<br><br>',
    '2) <b>EM HY Corporate — <a href="https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/BAMLEMHBHYCRPIOAS" target="_blank">BAMLEMHBHYCRPIOAS</a></b>. ',
    'The ICE BofA High Yield Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index OAS isolates the speculative grade tier of the EM corporate universe. It embeds both the EM country risk premium and the HY default risk premium simultaneously, making it the most volatile and highest-spread index in the EM credit family. This index sits at the intersection of global risk appetite, EM-specific country and currency dynamics, and speculative grade credit risk.'
  ),
  interpretation = "Plotting both EM tranches together reveals the IG/HY split within the EM corporate universe and how each responds to global and regional risk factors. The EM corporate all-in OAS responds to a combination of global risk appetite, US dollar dynamics, commodity prices, and country-specific fundamentals; the EM HY corporate OAS amplifies all of these factors given the additional speculative grade credit risk layer. Divergences between the two, a widening of EM HY relative to EM all-corporate, signal rising default concerns concentrated in the most leveraged EM issuers and are typically associated with commodity price declines, dollar strength, or idiosyncratic country stress. Comparing EM corporate spreads to equivalent US HY spreads reveals the additional EM risk premium the market is demanding at any given point in the cycle.")
```

```{=html}
<div class="perf-section">
```

```{r EM SPREAD, echo=FALSE, results='asis', warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
data <- get_series(name = c("ice_bofa_em_corp", "ice_bofa_em_hy_corp"))
div(style = "display: flex; justify-content: center;",
    produce_interactive_plot(df = data, sma = FALSE, title = "EM Corporate OAS — All EM Corporate vs. EM HY Corporate"))
```

```{=html}
</div>
```
